By doing conservation of angular momentum (sum of angular momenta = J*omega), I get the following. Let `b` be the robot's wheelbase over 2, `m` be the mass, `l` and `r` be the velocities of each side of the robot, `J` be the moment of inertia, and `omega` be the angular velocity of the robot.
```
-b * m * l + b * m * r = J * omega
b * m * (r - l) = J * omega
omega = b * m * (r - l) / J
```

The docstring for `groupby` should answer this question. We probably shouldn't link to a github discussion. Better to describe it succinctly here.

Fundamentally, I think `itertools.groupby` is more constrained because it has to support arbitrary iterables. You couldn't pass `(i for i in xrange(10))` into our custom `groupby`, but you can pass it into `itertools.groupby`.

And I think now that we're not evaluating the whole groupby result immediately in `groupByN`, we could use the built-in `itertools.groupby`, though I'm not necessarily suggesting this. The benefits we're getting are:

- Support for `len`
- Support for arbitrary evaluation of the nested generators returned by `groupByN` (though we're not currently using this)

I see where you're coming from here, but I think you misunderstand the intent of `kvalue_accuracy`.  We don't make particularly strong claims that the final kvalues are accurate to this level.  Rather we state in the doc about `kvalue_accuracy`:

>  Whenever there is some kind of approximation to be made in the calculation of a Fourier space value, the error in the approximation is constrained to be no more than this value times the total flux.

If we tried to make a stronger claim that the final k values won't have errors more than this much, then you'd always need to know what else is being convolved along with any given profile and take everything into account in any approximation being made.  This is clearly infeasible.

So I think it's fine if there are two places here where you make an approximation, each of which might have errors at this level.  I don't think it's necessary or appropriate to try to account for both approximations at once in the value of ksq_min that you use here.

@asqui Yes, Travis is running 3 test executions in parallel, one for each python version 27,34,37 but tests are run sequentially for each of them. That's a good thing, when it works and instead of going the dirty way of running them sequentially we should do the right thing and fix them. 

Also running tests in parallel will allow us to discover some real world scenarios that may not happen on a empty jira instance that is used exclusively for the purpose of one test. Is not quite a load test but if JIRA does not have atomic operations we should know it and adapt the library to make it more resilient to the server misbehaviours.

Based on the execution history I am inclined to believe that Jira is misbehaving and that is caching some results: example you may get a 5xx or 4xx error trying to create a resource (like a group or user) that was recently removed. By putting a sleep() or 1-2 seconds we may workaround this but I am not 100% sure yet as is very hard to get confirmation due to the flaky nature of the tests.

Still, I observed that these errors do not happen randomly over all the tests, when they happen they usually happen inside specific ones. 

@asqui It would be great if you can have a look as this is the most important issue with the library, one that is preventing us from starting to release often and to provide bug fixes. Feel free to use the `feature/travis` branch to test other changes.

If you want to simulate what happens with Travis but on your own machine you could try to run `detox` which is mainly running tox in parallel.

The entire idea is that we should support running tests in parallel and that's why we generate the `TestManager.jid` unique id, which is supposed to be unique per execution: we should never have two test executions that run in parallel that are using the same `jid`.

Again, I am talking about parallelism regarding the whole execution of the test suites, not tests themselves, these are sequential and even the order is important.

So this comment perhaps isn't the clearest, but we specifically need to filter out null *steps* here in order to use this index, since TensorSeriesStepIndex is only defined for non-null steps.

With the `step IS NOT NULL` filter, the inner query here can do a very efficient lookup of the min and max steps using exclusively the index (it doesn't even hit the actual table).  Without it, even with a `dtype IS NOT NULL` filter, it's reduced to doing a full scan over the entire range of steps.  So for a series of n steps, this degrades performance from something likely to be O(log n) or perhaps even O(1) (if pointers are kept to the start/end of the index) to O(n).

```bash
$ sqlite3 ~/scratch/tbdemo_fuller.sqlite "EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN SELECT MIN(step), MAX(step) FROM Tensors WHERE series = 123 AND step IS NOT NULL"
0|0|0|SEARCH TABLE Tensors USING COVERING INDEX TensorSeriesStepIndex (series=?)
$ sqlite3 ~/scratch/tbdemo_fuller.sqlite "EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN SELECT MIN(step), MAX(step) FROM Tensors WHERE series = 123 AND dtype IS NOT NULL"
0|0|0|SCAN TABLE Tensors
```

The impact isn't quite as drastic in all the other cases of making this change, but in general, applying the `step IS NOT NULL` filter should produce better performance since that enables sqlite to use this index when possible.

If I understand it correctly, with the change in this PR OpenThread code  will always provide an implementation for `otPlatLog()` (it may be `OT_TOOL_WEAKE` definition). As you mentioned the platform code can choose to override this if desired.

While this technically works, I personally think it would be better to force the platform developer to be explicit about it. My concern is that if the platform developer really intends to have her/his own `otPlatLog()` but forgets to implement/override the proper function (or  if the definition uses wrong arguments) the code may compile/build ok and the default internal OT implementation will be picked automatically. 

Here is my proposal:
We add a new config option to enable/disable default log output and also select where it is directed to.


````
/**
 * @def OPENTHREAD_SELECT_DEFAULT_LOG_OUTPUT
 * 
 * Define as 0 to disable any default log outputs. Platform should provide `otLogPlat()` implementation
 * Define as 1 for OpenThread to provide a default implementation for `otPlatLog()` which is
 * tied to either  NCP or CLI stream writes.
 * Define as 2 for OpenThread to use the `otPlatLog()` implementation from DEBUG uart feature 
 * (sa OPENTHREAD_ENABLE_DEBUG_UART)
 * 
 */
 ifndef OPENTHREAD_SELECT_DEFAULT_LOG_OUTPUT
 define OPENTHREAD_SELECT_DEFAULT_LOG_OUTPUT            0
 endif
````
This basically combines the `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_ENABLE_DEFAULT_LOG_OUTPUT` with the new `OPENTHREAD_ENABLE_DEBUG_UART_LOG`...

We might want `SendServerDataNotification() ` would be posted as a task, other than function call here.
In `Local::SendServerDataNotification()`, the verification conditions might still not be set yet when `SetRloc16()`, eg 
`mDeviceState = kDeviceStateRouter;` would not be set yet when `SetRloc16()` in `MleRouter::SetStateRouter()`, `kThreadError_InvalidState` would be returned. thus server data notification would not be sent out.

In fact for the rsdn border router, we might want the task be posted until `UpdateRoutes()`has been done at lease once in `HandleAdvertisement()` to ensure Leader is in its routing table. Because Router will find the neighbor only from its child set and routing table, obviously no valid neighbor would be found if no `UpdateRoutes()`.

This is addressing a (rare) corner case where the host (wpantund) has issued a new scan command and the we have not yet sent the event signaling the end of previous scan (no buffer space to send). In this case, the host has likely given up (timed out) on the previous scan and if we send a "end of scan" signal/event it can be confused as the end of current/new scan.  The "end of scan" message uses TID 0 (which is for sending async events) so there is no way for host/wpantund to associate it with a specific scan command. 

Note that we clear this flag only if the new scan command starts successfully.

In Python we don't really use getters and setters like in Java, because we can get the variable just by calling `self.aggregation_type` in this case. Usually when we use getter method, we add a `@property` decorator before the method signature, and the variable the getter returned is a private variable in this class which has a `_` before the variable name. Then with this decorated getter method, calling `self.aggregation_type` will return the value of the private variable `self._aggregation_type` in this class.

For more details look at this link: https://www.python-course.eu/python3_properties.php
